metadata.seed: Things? You know, I've been really sort of preoccupied and anxious a lot this weekend. My daughter got sick, so I had to take three days off of work to take care of her. And I'm starting to worry. She's been sick a lot since I've gone back to work, and she's in daycare, and I've had to take a lot of time off work, and I'm getting close to going over my allotted amount of days. And so I'm finding myself being very anxious about that and then feeling guilty because when I'm trying to take care of her, I'm always worrying about work. So.

patient.query: Things? You know, I've been really sort of preoccupied and anxious a lot this weekend. My daughter got sick, so I had to take three days off of work to take care of her. And I'm starting to worry. She's been sick a lot since I've gone back to work, and she's in daycare, and I've had to take a lot of time off work, and I'm getting close to going over my allotted amount of days. And so I'm finding myself being very anxious about that and then feeling guilty because when I'm trying to take care of her, I'm always worrying about work. So.

automatic_thoughts: I'm worried about my daughter's health
I'm going to run out of sick days
I feel guilty for worrying about work

llm_response.response: I hear how much pressure you’re feeling about both your daughter’s health and your work schedule. What evidence do you have that you’ll actually run out of sick days, and what evidence might suggest you could still manage? How might you balance caring for her while also addressing your work concerns in a way that feels less overwhelming?

patient.query: It’s just... I can’t shake this feeling that everything is falling apart. I keep picturing the worst-case scenario, like running out of sick days and feeling so torn between my responsibilities. Balancing it all feels impossible, and then I start to doubt myself, wondering if I’m doing enough for my daughter or if my work will see me as a failure. I guess I just feel so overwhelmed that it makes it hard to see any other way to handle things.

automatic_thoughts: wondering if I’m doing enough for my daughter
thinking my work will see me as a failure

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy everything feels right now—like the weight of work, family, and your own expectations all pressing down at once. It’s understandable that when you’re so overwhelmed, the worst outcomes seem to loom large. You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s okay to acknowledge that it’s a tough spot to be in.

patient.query: Yeah, it’s just... I often feel like I have to put on a brave face, like I can’t let anyone see how much it’s weighing on me. It’s like there’s this voice in my head telling me that I should be stronger, that I should just handle things better. But then I get this knot in my stomach, and I can’t help but worry that I’m not doing enough, you know? It’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I have to put on a brave face
I can’t let anyone see how much it’s weighing on me
I should just handle things better

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of pressure to keep everything together. What makes you think that showing how much it weighs on you would be a burden to others? And what evidence do you have that you’re not doing enough?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts of needing to be strong
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I guess I’ve always been taught to be strong, to not show weakness. It’s like I have this fear that if I open up, people will think I’m incapable or that I can't handle my life. As for not doing enough... I just see other people juggling everything so effortlessly, and it makes me feel like I must be falling short. I don’t know, maybe it's just comparing myself to a version of perfection that isn’t real.

automatic_thoughts: if I open up, people will think I’m incapable

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that opening up will make others see you as weak. What evidence do you have that people would actually think that? And how might someone you respect view your willingness to share?

patient.query: I don’t really know... I guess it’s just this fear that’s been there for a long time. I mean, I’ve seen others struggle and then just... bounce back, while I'm still stuck feeling this way. It’s almost like I’m afraid they’d think I’m not capable enough, like it would just confirm all those doubts I have about myself. But maybe... someone I respect would see it differently? They might understand that everyone has their moments, but I just can’t help

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Fear of not coping with treatment (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like seeing others bounce back has left you feeling stuck and uncertain. What evidence do you have that people actually think you’re not capable? If a trusted friend were to look at the same situation, what might they say about your strengths and progress?

patient.query: I mean, I know in my head that my friends care and wouldn’t judge me like that. But it’s this nagging feeling that just won’t go away, you know? Like when I see them succeeding, I think, “Why can’t I be like that?” I guess a friend might tell me I’ve been doing my best, that juggling work and family is no small feat. They might remind me of times I’ve handled tough situations pretty well, but it’s hard to hold

automatic_thoughts: Why can't I be like that?

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a persistent sense of inadequacy whenever you see your friends succeed. What evidence do you have that you’re not doing enough, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How could you remind yourself of the strengths you’ve already shown in juggling work and family?

patient.query: Yeah, it is like that. I just keep thinking of all the times I’ve messed up or felt overwhelmed. It feels more real than those moments when I’ve done well. Like, I know I’ve managed to keep things together most days, but it’s easy to forget those wins when I’m stuck in my head. It’s like I sort of discount them. I wish I could hold onto those moments longer, but they just seem to fade away when I start doubting myself.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where the negative moments feel more vivid than the positive ones. What evidence do you have that you’ve actually managed to keep things together? How might you remind yourself of those successes when you’re feeling doubtful?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that’s true. I mean, I can think of times when I’ve handled things well, like when I organized that family event and everyone had a great time. Or when I managed to meet a work deadline even though I was stressed. But in the moment, it’s like all those good memories just get overshadowed by the fear of messing up again. I don’t really know how to hold onto that evidence when I’m spiraling, though. It feels like those doubts

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re aware of those successful moments, yet the fear of messing up takes over when you’re stressed. What evidence do you have that you actually succeeded in those situations? How might you bring that evidence to mind when the fear starts to rise?

patient.query: I guess the evidence is there, like when my family told me how much they enjoyed the event, or how my manager appreciated my work on the project. It’s just... in those stressful moments, I feel like I’m back to square one, and all I can see are my mistakes. I really want to be able to remember those successes, but it’s like the fear blurs everything out, and I can’t find a way to reach for those good memories when I need them. It

automatic_thoughts: I can only see my mistakes
I can’t find a way to reach for good memories

llm_response.response: It sounds like those stressful moments really amplify the feeling that you’re stuck at the beginning again. What evidence do you have that you’re truly back to square one, and what evidence might suggest that you’ve actually moved forward? How might you notice or recall moments when you did succeed, even if they feel faint right now?

patient.query: I guess the evidence is there. Like when my family told me how much they enjoyed that event I organized or how my manager appreciated my work on the project. But in those stressful moments, I feel like I’m back to square one, and all I can see are my mistakes. I really want to remember those successes, but it’s like the fear blurs everything out, and I can’t find a way to reach for those good memories when I need them. It just makes everything feel heavier

automatic_thoughts: I can only see my mistakes
I can’t find a way to reach for good memories

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like stress makes you feel like you’re starting over and only notice the mistakes. What evidence do you have that this is the whole picture, and are there moments when you can still recall the positive feedback? How might you shift your focus to those successes when you feel overwhelmed?
